studies.title: A sequential dual-site repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation for major depressive disorder: A randomized clinical trial
studies.authors: Zhao YJ, Xiang S, Chen R, et al.
studies.abstract: Background: Standard high-frequency rTMS targeting the left dlPFC has modest antidepressant efficacy. The dmPFC, involved in affective processing and resting-state default mode network, represents an additional therapeutic target. Objective: To test whether sequential dual-site rTMS targeting both the left dlPFC and the dmPFC produces superior antidepressant effects in MDD. Methods: Randomized, double-blind, sham-controlled trial. MDD patients randomized to active dual-site rTMS (n=26) or sham (n=26). Protocol: 4-day accelerated treatment, 4 sessions/day; each session delivered sequential high-frequency rTMS to the left dlPFC followed by low-frequency rTMS to the dmPFC. Primary outcome: MADRS change from baseline to week 4. Secondary outcomes: response (≥50% MADRS reduction), remission (MADRS ≤10), resting-state fMRI connectivity. Results: At week 4, MADRS reductions significantly larger in the active group (p<0.001), with significant differences detectable from Day 4. Response rates: 57.69% (active) vs 23.08% (sham); remission rates: 38.46% vs 15.38%. Over 85% of responders maintained remission at 6-month follow-up. Resting-state fMRI showed increased dlPFC-frontoparietal network connectivity and decreased dmPFC-amygdalo-subcallosal cingulate connectivity associated with symptom improvement, indicating dissociable mechanisms at the two targets. Conclusions: Sequential dual-site rTMS targeting dlPFC and dmPFC produces rapid and sustained antidepressant effects with a cost-efficient 4-day protocol. This approach identifies a generalizable dual-network rTMS strategy for MDD.

studies.title: Application of cerebellar vermis theta-burst stimulation patterned repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation in patients with chronic schizophrenia
studies.authors: Lei S, Lei Q, Yang J, Peng H, Feng J
studies.abstract: Background: Chronic schizophrenia (CSZ) is associated with prominent negative symptoms and cognitive deficits poorly responsive to antipsychotic treatment. Cerebellar vermis stimulation has emerged as a novel target for rTMS in schizophrenia. Objective: To observe the therapeutic effects of cerebellar vermis theta-burst stimulation (TBS)-patterned rTMS in patients with CSZ. Methods: 114 CSZ patients admitted from February 2024 to July 2025 were randomly divided into two groups (n=57 each). Both groups received conventional antipsychotic treatment. The intervention group additionally underwent cerebellar vermis TBS-patterned rTMS; the control group received sham stimulation. Treatment lasted 4 weeks. Outcomes: neuroelectrophysiological measures (N2-P3 latency, N2-P3 amplitude, P300 latency, P300 amplitude) and cognitive domains (information processing speed, working memory, attention/vigilance, social cognition). Results: Neuroelectrophysiological measures and all assessed cognitive domains improved in both groups, with significantly greater improvements in the intervention group compared to controls. The incidence of adverse reactions did not differ significantly between groups. Conclusions: Cerebellar vermis TBS-patterned rTMS effectively stimulates neural activity in CSZ patients, alleviates negative and general symptoms, improves disease control, enhances cognitive and social functioning, and demonstrates high safety.

studies.title: Effectiveness of a Combined High-Definition tDCS-Primed Intermittent Theta Burst Stimulation Protocol Targeting the Cerebellar Vermis in Treatment-Resistant Schizophrenia: a Randomized Sham-Controlled Trial
studies.authors: Ranjan A, Garg S, Dhyani M
studies.abstract: Background: Approximately 60% of schizophrenia patients fail conventional antipsychotic treatment, constituting treatment-resistant schizophrenia (TRS). The cerebellar vermis plays a key role in cognitive and affective symptom regulation in schizophrenia. HD-tDCS can focally modulate cerebellar excitability and prime subsequent neuromodulation. Objective: To evaluate the safety and effectiveness of a combined HD-tDCS-primed iTBS protocol targeting the cerebellar vermis in TRS. Methods: Randomized, double-blind, sham-controlled trial. N=36 right-handed TRS patients (aged 18-59 years) randomized to active (n=18) or sham (n=18) groups. Active protocol: cathodal HD-tDCS (2 mA, 15 min) targeting the cerebellar vermis, followed 30 min later by iTBS (1800 pulses), 5 sessions/week for 2 weeks (10 sessions total). Assessments at baseline, Day 14, and Day 28: PANSS (Positive and Negative Syndrome Scale), SCoRS (Schizophrenia Cognition Rating Scale), MSAS (Modified Simpson-Angus Extrapyramidal Side Effects Scale), CGI-S, WHOQOL-BREF. Results: Intention-to-treat analysis showed significant between-group time × group interactions favoring the active group for PANSS total score, PANSS-negative subscale, and SCoRS. No significant between-group differences for positive symptoms (PANSS-positive). Minor side effects reported (headache, scalp tingling); no dropouts from adverse events. Conclusions: Combined cathodal HD-tDCS-primed iTBS targeting the cerebellar vermis is safe and modestly effective for overall psychopathology, negative symptoms, and cognitive functioning in TRS patients. This combined neuromodulation approach warrants further investigation in larger trials.

studies.title: Positive bias in brain and behaviour as a mechanism of transcranial magnetic stimulation depression treatment
studies.authors: Sarrazin V, Suen P, Cavendish B, et al.
studies.abstract: Background: The mechanisms by which TMS produces antidepressant effects remain unclear. Emotional processing biases are potential mediators. Objective: To test the hypothesis that TMS treatment changes emotional bias by increasing positive information processing. Methods: 49 patients with major depression received 20 daily sessions of open-label intermittent theta-burst stimulation (iTBS) targeting the left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC). Emotional bias was measured using behavioural tasks (emotional face processing) and functional MRI at baseline and after 8 days of treatment. Results: Increase in behavioural and neural measures of positive bias after one week predicted clinical response after four weeks of treatment. Behaviourally, clinical response was associated with interpreting ambiguous facial expressions as positive. Neurally, clinical improvement was related to increased fMRI response in the rostral anterior cingulate cortex (rACC) for positive versus negative emotional faces, and a positive bias in functional connectivity between rACC and posterior default mode network. Conclusions: Clinical response to iTBS treatment is associated with early increases in positive bias in emotional processing, potentially representing a neurocognitive mechanism of TMS antidepressant action neurally distinct from antidepressant medications.

studies.title: Consensus statement on the maintenance treatment of depression with rTMS in the Netherlands and Belgium
studies.authors: Dalhuisen I, Steenkamp S, Arns M, van Belkum SM, Dijkstra ESA, Egberink L, et al.
studies.abstract: Background: After an acute course of rTMS for depression, patients who respond face the question of how to prevent relapse. Maintenance strategies are applied clinically but lack standardization. Objective: The Dutch-Flemish Brain Stimulation Foundation convened an expert panel to review the literature and formulate consensus recommendations on maintenance treatment with rTMS for depression in the Netherlands and Belgium. Methods: A systematic PubMed search (up to June 2025) identified 22 studies reporting original data on maintenance rTMS for depression. A clinical survey was conducted to inventory current practice. An expert panel reviewed and discussed the evidence and formulated consensus recommendations. Results: Three main maintenance approaches were identified: (1) Tapering — gradual reduction of session frequency after acute treatment; (2) Maintenance rTMS — fixed-interval regular sessions; (3) Retreatment — re-initiating a full rTMS course upon relapse. Findings: Single-session maintenance protocols show mixed results. Clustered maintenance protocols (typically 5 sessions per month delivered over 2-3 consecutive days) demonstrate the most consistent benefits, though controlled trial data are limited. Retreatment with rTMS is effective in most patients who previously responded, often requiring fewer sessions than the initial course. The panel recommends individualized maintenance planning based on response to acute treatment, relapse history, and patient preference. Conclusions: Evidence supports maintenance rTMS to prevent depressive relapse, with clustered protocols showing the most promise. Standardized trials are needed. These consensus recommendations provide practical guidance for clinicians.

studies.title: A cell type-specific mechanism driving the rapid antidepressant effects of transcranial magnetic stimulation
studies.abstract: Background: Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) is an emerging treatment for depression and other brain disorders, but its therapeutic mechanism remains poorly understood. Objective: To identify the cell-type-specific neural mechanism by which accelerated intermittent theta burst stimulation (aiTBS) produces rapid antidepressant effects. Methods: A mouse model of rTMS with improved clinical face validity was developed. The chronic unpredictable stress model was used to induce depression-like behavioral deficits. Fiber photometry was used to monitor in vivo neural activity of intratelencephalic (IT) and pyramidal tract (PT) projecting neurons in the dorsomedial prefrontal cortex (dmPFC) during and after aiTBS. Dendritic spine density was assessed following aiTBS. Results: aiTBS drives distinct patterns of neural activity in IT and PT neurons in the dmPFC. Only IT neurons exhibit persistently increased activity during aiTBS and during subsequent depression-related behaviors. aiTBS reverses stress-related loss of dendritic spines on IT neurons but not PT neurons, demonstrating cell type-specific effects. Conclusions: IT neurons in the dmPFC selectively mediate the antidepressant effects of aiTBS. These findings provide a mechanistic basis for understanding how TMS produces rapid antidepressant effects, with implications for optimizing future clinical TMS protocols targeting specific neural circuits.

studies.title: Efficacy of 5 × 5 accelerated versus conventional repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) for treatment-resistant depression
studies.authors: Apostol MR, Valles TE, Corlier J, Leuchter MK, Young AS
studies.abstract: Background: Accelerated rTMS protocols delivering multiple daily sessions over fewer days may reduce treatment burden while maintaining efficacy. This study compared 5×5 accelerated rTMS (5 sessions/day × 5 consecutive days) to conventional once-daily rTMS in patients with treatment-resistant depression (TRD). Methods: 175 TRD patients were treated at a UCLA academic medical center. Conventional rTMS (n=135): at least 30 sessions, once/day, 5 days/week for 6 weeks. Accelerated 5×5 rTMS (n=40): personalized iTBS (piTBS, n=25) or individualized resonant frequency stimulation (RF, n=15), 5 sessions/day for 5 consecutive days (≥25 sessions total). Primary outcome: PHQ-9 depression score changes from baseline to end-of-treatment and 2-4 week follow-up. Results: Both groups showed significant reductions in depressive symptoms. No statistically significant difference in overall outcome between conventional and accelerated treatment schedules. Response rates at treatment completion: 58.5% (conventional) vs 37.5% (accelerated). Remission rates: 24% vs 22.5%. PHQ-9 median split for 5×5 cohort: patients in the top half (higher baseline severity) achieved 69% symptom improvement by Day 5, while bottom half showed only 8% improvement by Day 5 but 36% significant improvement at 2-4 week follow-up. Conclusions: 5×5 accelerated rTMS shows comparable overall efficacy to conventional once-daily rTMS for TRD. Full benefit of accelerated treatment may not be captured at Day 5 but continues to emerge over 2-4 weeks post-treatment. These results support 5×5 as a time-efficient alternative to standard protocols.
